data.title: Snowflake: Little Opportunity At Current Valuation
data.description:
Even though Snowflake, Inc.'s (SNOW) fundamentals were strong and the outlook was positive as discussed in my previous article, I only rated the stock as a hold. This was due to the massive valuation premium and it led me to conclude that Snowflake wasn't for the faint of heart. So far, the stock has actually held up fine as there has been gains of around 5% since then. With their most recent earnings having come out in early December and some significant developments having happened since then, I believe now is the time to give an update for readers.

Below, it is shown that there is some top line growth slowdown for Snowflake but very strong RPO increases indicate that there isn't a demand problem for their AI cloud solutions. Furthermore, headcount additions and deals show that the company is aggressive pursuing the opportunity at hand and prioritizing long term growth at this point. While the company is overall well-positioned, the valuation remains at highly elevated levels and so at this time, I believe a reiteration of my hold rating is appropriate.

Cloud Activity Has Been Robust

In my previous update on Snowflake, I discussed a very similar infographic to the one above. Back then the company did a comparison between April 2020 and July 2025's AI Data Cloud sharing activity and there was a stark contrast. In their most recent earnings presentation, they did this comparison again, this time with October 2025 results and if you look back to my September article, you can see that activity has strengthened notably again in just a few months. Investors that are concerned about an AI bubble are worried that there will be an excess amount of infrastructure. I believe those concerns are quite clearly misplaced as the strong activity at companies like Snowflake show that real world demand for AI solutions is in fact booming in a way that suggests there isn't a slowdown.

Revenue Growth Moderates

Snowflake's top line growth rate did decelerate modestly from the previous quarter but at 28.75%, I would say that business activity has remained in a solid position. In the above chart, it is shown that growth is much slower than back in early CY2023 but that may be forgivable as a larger growth base eventually takes its toll.

In general though, the company's revenues have been on a steady uptrend and in their FY2026 Q3, it hit $1.213 billion. That surprised on the upside by a good margin of $28.9 million and so there isn't too much to complain about in these results. Snowflake's preferred metric is product revenue but for Q3, the growth rate was also 29%, which is a modest deceleration from the previous quarter. Like always, looking at a company's RPO may be an even better indication of where demand is at currently.

RPOs Show That Demand Is A Non-Issue

In my previous article, RPOs were also discussed and there was a 33% increase YoY. Well, for Q3 they had an even stronger 37% increase and so despite the drop in the top line growth rate, demand for their cloud services remains strong and has perhaps even strengthened. Again, I would say AI infrastructure bubble fears are mostly without substance as the strong demand in the cloud shows that real world applications are significant. In the case of Snowflake, clients are essentially lining up. With RPO growth accelerating, there is a good case for increasing the rate at which infrastructure gets built.

Sales Growth Is Clearly A Priority

During a time where some tech companies are reducing headcount and downsizing, Snowflake is clearly headed in the opposite direction. While this may weigh a bit on near term profitability, it does suggest the company sees growth as a priority right now. As you can see in the above chart, total employee headcount increased by 15% to 9,023. Note that sales and marketing employees headcount rose by 26% to show that the company is aggressive pursuing market share. That said, I would say the relatively mild increase in R&D headcount is a little disappointing. There was a just 5% increase to 2,403 employees and for a young cloud company I expected a larger emphasis on innovation.

Margins Show Improvement

Firstly, gross margin was unchanged from the prior year period and so cost control has remained very consistent. Secondly, the improvement in operating margin is impressive given the notable increase in headcount as discussed above. Even with the added profitability pressures of an expanding workforce, Snowflake was still able to expand their operating margin by a quite impressive 5 percentage points. This indicates that they are not over hiring and that productivity of the workforce has been strong. Also shown above is their adjusted FCF margin. With cash ultimately being king, this increase in cash generation ability is obviously a good sign for investors.

In terms of the bottom line, Snowflake reported an adjusted EPS of $0.35 and that beat analyst expectations by $0.04 to show good performance. In the above chart, you can see that EPS growth also decelerated from the previous quarter but at 75% there isn't much to complain about. We'll discuss their guidance in just a minute but as you can see consensus estimates call for some rockiness in the next few quarters. That isn't that concerning as of now with demand shown to be more than solid.

Deals Galore

Another sign that Snowflake is aggressively pursuing the opportunity at hand is the amount of deals that were recently announced. In early December, the company expanded a generative AI partnership with Accenture (ACN) and this will ultimately lead to better outcomes for clients.

On the same day, they also announced a $200 million deal with Anthropic which is of course one of the leading AI startups in the world today. This partnership will reportedly expand the integration of Claude into Snowflake's Cortex AI platform and this will allow enterprise clients to access agentic AI technology.

Beyond just partnerships, there were reports in late December that Snowflake was in talks to acquire AI app monitoring startup, Observe. As Manshi Mamtora, CFA writes, "the acquisition would bolster Snowflake's capabilities in full-stack observability, accelerating AI workflows for troubleshooting and reducing enterprise costs."

I'm not going to get into the details of these three deals but I believe this brief overview shows that Snowflake is definitely on the offensive and actively seeking out opportunities to grow its reach in AI.

Q4 Outlook

We'll focus on their Q4 guidance with just one quarter to report for the fiscal year but it should be mentioned that they raised full year revenue expectations after a good Q3 and that's of course a welcomed sign.

For Q4 specifically, they are projecting product revenues in the range of $1.195 billion to $1.2 billion. That would be a 27% YoY increase and while that's still quite strong, it should be noted that there was a 29% growth rate for Q3. Again, however, given the acceleration in RPOs, demand is unlikely to be a problem. At this point, a more reasonable worry would be how efficiently the company is able to convert RPOs into revenue.

For operating margin, Snowflake expects it to be at 7% which would be a notable dip from the year ago quarter's 9%. Therefore, the increase in headcount may be catching up to the company in terms of near term financial performance.

Valuation Continues To Be High

The forward P/E ratio for Snowflake has expanded modestly from 188.27 during my previous article to a current reading of 192.30. In my previous update, I found the company's fundamentals to be strong but the bull thesis fell apart since I viewed the valuation as excessive. I believe this is the case again now.

Snowflake's top line growth could have been a bit better but overall the key takeaway is that there is nothing to suggest that clients are turning away. As stated numerous times now, RPOs are strong and with the company ambitiously pursuing growth, they are overall well positioned.

However, at the same time, Seeking Alpha shows that the stock's non-GAAP forward P/E ratio is at a nearly 650% premium to the IT sector as of this writing. The company represents an exciting part of the AI development story in the U.S. and globally. However, I would say that the good but not extraordinary enough fundamentals makes this valuation a bit questionable.

Catalysts/Risks

A potential catalyst that could make Snowflake stock a buy would be a meaningful increase in synergies. As discussed earlier, the company has expanded important partnerships and is looking at a strategic acquisition. If these developments lead to a significant acceleration in growth, which is definitely possible given that Snowflake is one of the leaders in data analytics and cloud AI globally, I would say that the valuation can be viewed as more opportunistic.

Conversely, the greatest potential risk for the stock's performance would be short-termism. Synergies take time but in the near term, the company is facing decelerating top line growth and possible margin erosion with headcount rising. Combining that with a high current valuation and that may create incentives for traders and such to take profits and so while the aggressive pursuit of opportunity may be beneficial for the long term, near term weakness in results creates risk to the downside.

Conclusion

After today's analysis, I have come to basically the same conclusion as in my previous article. The company has overall solid fundamentals right now and the opportunity in front of them is large. Management seems to be prioritizing growth over near term returns right now and that is by no means a bad thing. What is a worry is the valuation. As discussed above, extraordinary fundamentals are needed to justify a forward P/E that's quite close to 200 and that's where Snowflake does fall a bit short. That said, ultimately this company has a bright future ahead and so I've decided to keep my hold rating for now.

data.title: Thungela Resources: Dividend Expectations Might Leave Investors In The Ash
data.description:
Intro

Thungela Resources (TNGRF) is a standalone, South-Africa based thermal coal company since having been spun out of Anglo American in 2021 where it was the “dirty” coal division of the latter.

Even though the chart of Thungela looks boring on the surface, in fact the price movements were anything but boring. I can exactly remember how the South-Africa and London listed stock was first sold off hard, as no one wanted to keep the dirty ashes of a cleaner and forward-looking Anglo American. Thungela stock changed course relatively quickly, supported by exploding thermal coal prices — the commodity it produces — multiplying in price by almost 20x from the deepest bottom to its all-time high.

Since then, in light of crashing coal prices, the stock has given up most of its gains, being stuck in a painful and enduring sideways range for almost three years now.

Thungela was mainly discovered by the broader investor base after it paid high-caloric dividends. Personally, I can remember having received in one year 133% on my invested capital in dividends.

However, with weakening business results and a few operational challenges, the dividends have been burning out slowly over time. Next March, with the announcement of the full-year 2025 results, management will also announce the next dividend. My fear and core of this analysis is, expectations for a big dividend might go up in smoke, resulting in a small and strongly reduced payout that feels like dusty leftovers.

As the stock is looking already dirt-cheap, I do not give it a sell rating, but a hold. However, Thungela is not a buy for me either, as I expect disappointing results together with a meager dividend, potentially sending the stock lower.

Burned out financials — what to expect

Thungela Resources in early December held a pre-close call to discuss its so-far performance for 2025. I must admit, I am not impressed, despite some positives with which I want to start, before quickly transitioning to what I do not like. By the way, here is also the published press release, but it is only pure text, hence I am not posting screenshots of text into my text.

To be clear, the current environment is very challenging amid soft coal prices. The macro picture remains challenging, despite reports about record-high coal consumption, driven by high demand in Asia. However, the big picture is that the market remains oversupplied, respectively “well supplied”.

Or as Thungela said it themselves (bold by me):

Seaborne thermal coal prices remain depressed on the back of weak demand, caused mainly by the uncertainty around the impact of tariﬀs and lower gas prices, while the supply discipline that was expected, has not yet fully materialised. Demand from China and India, the largest importers of thermal coal, remained below expectations for most of the year, as a result of increasing domestic production and support for the growth of alternative energy sources.

Turning to Thungela, thermal coal production exceeded initial expectations, with South African export saleable volumes projected at 13.7 million tonnes, surpassing the guidance of 12.8 to 13.6 million tonnes. I am saying “projected”, because the data goes until end of November 2025. This surprising “beat” was driven by successful ramp-ups at Annea (formerly named Elders) as well as strong contributions from operations such as Mafube, as the CFO laid out during the trading update.

The Transnet Freight Rail issues (leading to lower exports) seem to have turned the corner. Performance improved, achieving an annualized rate of 56.6 million tonnes through November. Costs for the South Africa operations are expected to come in below guidance.

This is where the good news end.

Australia's Ensham mine, is on track to produce 3.8 million tonnes, within its guidance, however, only on the lower end of the range of 3.7–4.1 million tonnes. During the H1 2025 results, management reported “geological challenges”, leading to lower-quality coal output. Thungela took control of the mine with an initial 85% stake in September 2023. The remaining part was bought in 2025 as announced in December 2024.

In this latest update, the CFO said the lower-quality coal was successfully marketed, but I do not see this Australian mine as a successful diversification asset. To the contrary, not only was I negatively surprised about the geology issues, but during the first-half discussion, production costs jumped to ZAR 1,694 per tonne (equivalent to about $100–105 per tonne at current rates) — dangerously close to the spot price.

South African coal sells a bit below this price, the Australian version a bit above — but as a broad yardstick, we can say that being close to the commodity price is not favorable. Especially, as coal is often sold at discounts, first and foremost if the quality is low(er) like we had here.

We can say that Thungela is trying to compete the best it can.

Management reaffirmed its commitment to a minimum 30% payout of adjusted operating free cash flow as dividends. I am not a fan on adjusted figures and strange metrics like “operating free cash flow”, which make it tougher to estimate what’s coming. But there are a few signs that the coming results will not be strong, potentially leading to a disappointing dividend declaration.

Thungela aims to create shareholder returns via investments in life-extension projects, which are nearing completion, allowing to reduce capital spend in the future. But this alone is likely not enough. Headwinds from softer thermal coal markets are pressing. As explained in the update, the South African Richards Bay benchmark for local thermal coal averaged only $89.63 per tonne through November 2025 (down from approximately $105 per tonne in 2024). The Newcastle benchmark, relevant for the Australian operations, averaged $105 per tonne (versus $135 previously). Subdued demand from key importers like China and India, and on top a surprisingly stronger rand, eroded competitiveness, squeezing margins.

The result is a looming impairment on property, plant, and equipment, as commodity prices and exchange rates no longer support current carrying values. Concretely, from the press release (bold by me):

The Group will undertake the annual assessment of the value of property, plant and equipment (PPE) ahead of finalising the 2025 annual financial results. The assessment requires significant judgement in relation to forward-looking market-related conditions, specifically benchmark coal prices and foreign exchange rates, which are currently at levels that does not support the carrying value of our PPE balances.

This underscores the danger and risk of relying heavily on book value metrics to spot “cheap” (commodity) stocks. Without a doubt, Thungela stock looks tempting with its tangible book value being significantly below 1x, closer to 0.5x. But post the likely impairment, this number will shoot higher. How much higher, we don’t know, yet. But the stock looks cheaper than it is in the current macro environment.

Coming to my last point that is likely of most interest to readers, the dividend. Cash generation appears poised for a weak outcome. “Adjusted operating free cash flow” according to management remained positive, which is great. But it relied substantially on ZAR 1.2 billion from expiring forex hedges, i.e. a one-time effect that does not come from the core business as such. This tailwind is unlikely to repeat, respectively put another way, in my understanding should be adjusted out from the figure that determines the dividend.

Here’s why I am so skeptical about the dividend, directly from management (bold and italic by me):

In line with prior periods, several transactions that typically conclude in December are expected to impact our 31 December 2025 net cash 6 position. These include the green fund contribution in Australia and the provisional tax payments in South Africa and Australia. Our net cash balance at 31 December 2025 is accordingly expected to range between R4.9 billion and R5.2 billion. The net cash balance range includes approximately R1.2 billion, on a full-year basis, of cash generated relating from foreign exchange derivatives.

In other words, the year will likely leave the business near cash neutral at prevailing prices, factoring out the foreign exchange gain from hedges. Year-end net cash is forecast at ZAR 4.9–5.2 billion, perilously close to (or potentially below) the company’s preferred buffer. Historically, management has viewed around ZAR 5 billion as appropriate in softer conditions. This doesn’t mean, cash can drop below this figure temporarily, but it would be a very aggressive maneuver to appease shareholders with short-term pleasures (the dividend), at a time when the core business might not even generate positive cash flow.

By the way, cash (orange) has been slowly burning out, while payables rose from the ashes over time. This is not supportive for a high dividend either. Factoring out the one-time hedging gain, the cash buffer via my estimates halved since summer.

My take is that Thungela has already burned through its safety buffer, potentially due to underestimating the current environment. The last sentence is highly speculative from my side, but the numbers allow for such an interpretation.

In essence, Thungela navigates a tightening rope: on the surface, it looks like a boring period with everything under control, but fading coal prices amid structural shifts (weak demand, high supply) risk capsizing valuations through asset impairments and leaving shareholders hoping for fat dividends only with ashes, if the market fails to rebound. December 2025 did not show a strong rebound in coal prices, hence I would not plan with a big payout. In an extremely case, the dividend would be cut to zero to preserve cash — likely crashing the stock. But more likely is a symbolic payout, claiming to return cash to shareholders even in tough times.

My vote is a hold with the tendency for a sell — however, I would not buy this stock. I remain on the sidelines.

Risks to my Thesis

The biggest risk are sharply rising thermal coal prices which would strongly increase margins and financial flexibility. Currently, there are no signs for this to be in the making.

With a market cap of less than a billion USD and being an independent company, Thungela could be acquired at a premium to the current price, offering a quick price return for shareholders.

Conclusion

I am not convinced of Thungela. The macro environment is weak and the company is at risk of posting highly disappointing results in March, together with a dividend significantly below expectations.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Don't Mistake Speculation For Strength: The Mirage Of Small-Cap 'Junk' Outperformance
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA and Brian Manby, CFA

U.S. small-cap equities have defied traditional logic in 2025. The companies investors have typically prized for their prudence, those with steady earnings and consistent dividends, have lagged behind the more speculative corners of the market. Instead, firms with no profits and no payouts have been leading performance charts, marking a striking reversal of the patterns that dominated much of the post-global financial crisis era.

  • Risk Appetite Revival: Speculative small caps are benefiting from renewed enthusiasm for growth optionality rather than cash flow discipline.
  • Liquidity and Leverage Effects: Easy financial conditions and the return of retail flows have amplified momentum in unprofitable names.1
  • Valuation Divergence: Dividend payers and profitable small-cap firms now trade at unusually wide valuation discounts, potentially setting up a mean-reversion opportunity.

Figure 1 captures a rare market inversion: In 2025, unprofitable and non-dividend-paying small caps are sharply outperforming their higher-quality peers. After years when fundamentals drove returns, investor sentiment has pivoted toward speculative growth. The data underscores a risk-on rotation, liquidity-driven exuberance and a potential setup for future mean reversion.

Figure 1: Something New under the Sun?

Sources: WisdomTree, MSCI, FactSet, as of 11/3/25. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index. Profitable (unprofitable) small caps are defined as constituents of the MSCI USA Small Cap Index that had positive (negative) trailing 12-month earnings as of the most recent calendar year-end. Returns represent the weighted average year-to-date and calendar year returns among constituents using index weights as of the beginning of the return period. Annualized returns are calculated based upon calendar year and year-to-date 2025 returns, which individually use beginning-of-evaluation period weights.

Despite 2025's surge in "junk" small caps, longer-term results, shown in figure 2, reaffirm the value of fundamentals. Over the past six years, profitable and dividend-paying companies have delivered the strongest compounded gains, while unprofitable firms remain in negative territory. The data highlights that short-term speculation fades, but quality compounds over time.

Figure 2: Longer Horizons May Look Different from Shorter Periods in Small-Cap Performance

Sources: WisdomTree, MSCI, FactSet, as of 11/3/25. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index. Profitable (unprofitable) small caps are defined as constituents of the MSCI USA Small Cap Index that had positive (negative) trailing 12-month earnings as of the most recent calendar year-end. Returns represent the weighted average year-to-date and calendar year returns among constituents using index weights as of the beginning of the return period. Annualized returns are calculated based upon calendar year and year-to-date 2025 returns, which individually use beginning-of-evaluation period weights.

Conclusion: A Look at Two Exchange-Traded Funds Focused on U.S. Small Caps

The most widely referenced benchmark for U.S. small-cap equity performance is the Russell 2000 Index. The iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) is designed to track the total return performance, before fees, of this benchmark. As of September 30, 2025, it had roughly 28% of its weight in companies that had negative earnings over the prior 12 months.2

The WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Dividend Growth Fund (DGRS) is designed to track the total return performance of an index focused on higher-quality small-cap companies that all pay dividends.3 They also have relatively higher return on equity and return on assets measures.

Therefore, we can look at:

  • IWM as a broad-market benchmark with significant exposure to more speculative small-cap stocks.
  • DGRS as a more focused strategy on U.S. small caps that emphasizes the higher-quality angle.

For those investors considering that the speculative rally may end, we wanted to focus on two of the following figures. Figure 3a indicates the valuation discount, on multiple metrics, of DGRS relative to IWM.

Figure 3b indicates the return on equity and return on assets of DGRS versus IWM.

Figure 3a: DGRS Shows a Valuation Discount on Multiple Measures against IWM

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, Morningstar, with data as of 9/30/25.

Figure 3b: DGRS Shows Significantly Higher Quality Metrics than IWM

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, Morningstar, with data as of 9/30/25

If the trend shifts back to rewarding higher-quality small caps, we think that DGRS may be particularly well positioned.

Figure 4: Additional Information

Sources: Specific Fund pages on the WisdomTree and iShares website, with AUM figures as of 11/5/25.

1 Source: "Small-cap stocks gain momentum: A new era of retail-driven growth and earnings optimism," AInvest, 10/25/25.

2 Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, Morningstar, with data accessed as of 11/6/25 within WisdomTree's Fund Comparison tool.

3 This index is the WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Dividend Growth Index.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors and/or smaller companies increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Brian Manby, CFA, Senior Associate, Investment Strategy

Brian Manby joined WisdomTree in October 2018 as an Investment Strategy Analyst. He is responsible for assisting in the creation and analysis of WisdomTree’s model portfolios, as well as helping support the firm’s research efforts. Prior to joining WisdomTree, he worked for FactSet Research Systems, Inc. as a Senior Consultant, where he assisted clients in the creation, maintenance and support of FactSet products in the investment management workflow. Brian received a B.A. as a dual major in Economics and Political Science from the University of Connecticut in 2016. He is holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst designation.

data.title: MP Materials Is A Rare Earths Leader But Lacks Margin Of Safety At $60
data.description:
MP Materials (MP) is a leading rare earths producer in the Western Hemisphere. I previously issued a strong buy rating on MP at $17.96/share in early 2024 when there was scant interest in this stock.

When rare earths became a contentious point between the US and China in mid-2025, MP's stock price surged to a high of $100/share in Oct-25.

On August 9, 2025, I issued a hold rating on MP, citing that its valuation has more than priced in its foreseeable future profitability.

Since then, after hitting the $100 high on Oct-25, MP’s stock price has fallen nearly 50% in two months to close the year at $50/share.

Now may be a good time to take another look at MP now that the initial furor has died down. Is it time to buy the dip or wait?

1. Recap of events in rare earths in the past few months

2025 has been a rollercoaster year for rare earths:

  • In April 2025, China imposed export controls on rare earths as part of the US-China trade spat. Since then, China and the US have repeatedly held discussions to maintain the flow of rare earths out of China.
  • By late June 2025, reports hinted that China would allow the flow of rare earths but keep its hand on the tap.
  • In October 2025, China further introduced export controls on the technologies and downstream linkages related to rare earths.
  • In early November, following President Trump’s meeting with President Xi in South Korea, there was another cooling of trade tensions as part of a broader 1-year easing package. While there have been flare-ups and tensions now and then, overall the US and China appear to be maintaining a fragile status quo. For example, China resumed soybean purchases while the US allowed semiconductor equipment to be exported to China.
  • Overall, it appears rare earths will be a contentious point between the US and China for a long period of time. As analysts have suggested, China has found a magic lever that offers strong negotiating power with the US, and it’s unlikely to give up that magic lever easily. However, so far it appears the initial furor has died down and tensions are simmering rather than boiling.

2. Recap of MP's recent financials

As shown below, MP's financial performance in the past two quarters has been lackluster; revenues are treading water, and operating income is negative as NdPr prices languish at low levels. However, MP's main potential is its planned refining capacity (i.e., turning rare earth ores into magnets) rather than its current operations. According to MP's Q3-2025 earnings call, they estimate magnet production will start by mid-2026. Though MP is oft-quoted as the only game in town with regard to rare earth refining in North America, is the current valuation justified?

3. Is the current market cap still overvalued?

MP’s market cap has declined a little since my Aug-25 piece arguing it may be overvalued ($12 bn market cap then versus $10 bn market cap right now). From two perspectives, MP may still be overvalued:

  • NdPr prices (the blue line below corresponding to the axis on the left) are up a bit in 2025 ($84/kg in Dec. 2025) compared to the low of $50/kg in 2024 but still nowhere near the 2022 highs of $160+/kg. NdPr prices have reacted in a much more muted fashion than MP’s stock price would suggest, and if there is not a significant jolt from new catalysts, NdPr prices could just continue hovering at these levels.

    • MP’s offtake deal with the DoD of 10,000 tons per year at $110/kg is decidedly less profitable than it may appear on paper. For example, one of China’s largest rare earth conglomerates, China Northern Rare Earth Group, has a TTM net profit margin of 5%. Even during the peak years of 2022, its net profit margin was about 15%. So 10,000 tons at $110/kg is $1.1 billion a year; assuming a net margin of 15% a year, this would only be $165 million a year. Let’s say MP earns super high net margins of 30% because it is the only game in town in North America and because of subsidies from the US government or companies; then it would earn $330 million net profit a year. Compared with a market cap of $10 billion, this would still be a P/E of 27, assuming super high margins and under heavy subsidy.
    • Also, Europe is looking to diversify its own rare earth capacities and not switch dependency on rare earths from China to the US. As major industrial countries seek to control their own supply chains, MP’s potential total addressable market shrinks as well to perhaps the Americas rather than necessarily the entire Western world.
    • MP's stock price is extremely volatile: In the mid-2020s, it was about $10/share, hitting nearly $60/share in 2022 and falling to near $10/share in 2024. Despite the long-term importance of this sector, the 2023-2024 decline of 80+% in this stock shows that buying at peak interest could face large drawdowns even if the price eventually recovers.

4. Outlook going forward

At current prices, I can see things going both ways: it’s possible that new news significantly jolts MP higher, and it’s also possible that the furor about rare earths dies down once this becomes a long-term problem and decidedly less interesting.

I currently lean towards the latter view: the market may slowly realize that MP’s buildout of its refining capacities will take a long time, and even with DoD subsidies and price floors, MP might not be able to earn back its market cap through discounted cash flows. Meanwhile, NdPr prices hover near current levels as China continues supplying the US. As that realization gradually sinks in, MP’s stock price may grind lower for an indefinite period of time until the next major flare-up in tensions between the US and China on trade or other issues.

Risks to neutral thesis

MP could have potentially huge upside if US and China trade or other geopolitical tensions spiral out of control again. Then the MP’s value would not just be in terms of a DCF analysis but would be a vital strategic asset for the US. However, things do not seem to be headed in that direction right now.

Conclusion:

At current prices I would view any purchase of MP to be very speculative. I would view it as a potential buy with more margin of safety if the stock price gradually declined to significantly lower prices (e.g., $20-$30/share) as market enthusiasm for this sector cools. Another major catalyst would be needed for MP’s stock price to surge, and right now no such catalyst is evident.

data.title: Skyworks Solutions: Apple Concentration Discount Keeps Shares Cheap, But I Expect This To Change
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to revisit one of my smaller positions in my portfolio to see what has been happening since my last article over a year ago, where I maintained a buy rating as diversification efforts looked very promising.

Skyworks Solutions, Inc. (SWKS) continues to suffer from the concentration discount of Apple, but diversification efforts are going rather strongly, and I predict that over the next couple of years, the discount will go away, much quicker than Apple is going to replace its products.

I am maintaining a buy rating and will be looking to build a solid position going forward.

Briefly On Performance

Looking at SWKS’s revenue trend, we can see that it was not the best in terms of growth in 2024, to say the least. The Apple (AAPL) content losses made a significant dent in the company’s operations, which was expected since its revenues are very dependent on Apple to maintain the relationship they had, but alas, that was not the case, as Tim Cook decided to reduce Apple’s reliance on SWKS by dual-sourcing and making in-house development efforts.

We can also see that in the first quarter of 2025, the company’s fortunes began to turn around as its sales bottomed at the end of ’24, with mobile seeing decent recovery sequentially and y/y, driven by stronger iPhone sell-through, and some notable Android wins, like the Google Pixel 10. Additionally, in broad market terms, we saw inventories normalizing by mid-’25, with traction gained in Wi-Fi 7 and the automotive sector, reducing its reliance on Apple, thus increasing diversification, although minimally, it’s a start.

Looking at the company’s efficiency and profitability over the same period, we can see that these have not done well at all. Margins across the board have taken a massive hit, and it doesn’t look like they are ready to come back up yet. What gives? It all stems from Apple’s content erosion and unfavorable product mixes, which don’t allow for fixed costs to be distributed very efficiently.

Onto the company’s other important metrics, ROA, ROE, and ROTC unsurprisingly came down over the same period, also, since its bottom line dropped by 700bps. It seems that the concentration on one customer, like Apple, wreaks havoc on how efficiently the management is utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder capital. There’s no efficiency. I will be monitoring these metrics going forward.

In the latest quarter, which was Q4 ’25, SWKS reported sales of $1.1B, up around 8% y/y, also which topped estimates by around $50m. Q4 Non-GAAP EPS came in at $1.76, beating estimates by 23 cents, and generated a decent amount of operating cash flow, to the tune of $1.3B for the quarter. It is still very early to see if the sales actually bottomed, but it is a promising sign.

Now, let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. Cash and equivalents stand at around $1.39B, and debt at around $1B. Interest expense for the quarter was a little under $7m, so it’s not an issue to keep a billion in debt on its books since it has plenty of cash to cover, as well as it generated around $144m in free cash flow this past quarter alone and $1.3B for the year, as I already mentioned.

So overall, I would say operations aren’t looking quite as nice as one would hope, but the strong cash flow and balance sheet will allow it to survive for many years to come. With time, I believe the company will be able to acquire more companies to diversify away from reliance on Apple, which will attract a lot more investors. It’ll take time, but I don’t doubt the management will execute the right strategy.

Comments On The Outlook

Apple Concentration Risk

Investors are not the biggest fans of such a concentrated operation as SWKS. It is only a matter of time before Tim finally has a decent product that can fully replace Skyworks’s products. I hope by then, SWKS’s reliance on Apple performance is going to be minimal. However, there’s no denying that this concentration is putting a massive discount on the company’s shares and overall performance. Every time I recommend it as a buy, it continues to go the other direction. It’s slightly frustrating since I have a small position currently, but not as bad since it is still rather small, which means once the sentiment does turn around and the company manages to diversify away from Apple, investors will be quick to jump on the bandwagon. At some point, the company is going to become so cheap that the concentration risk is going to be fully priced in, and from then on, we will see the trend turning around.

The Diversification Strategy

Wi-Fi 7 And Wi-Fi 8

These two represent the company’s core diversification plays, targeting enterprise and home access points, routers, and industrial IoT rather than Apple’s handsets that use proprietary Wi-Fi products. Wi-Fi 7 drove the Broad Market segment to around 35% of total revenue in the latest reports, and the guidance for next quarter shows the segment to reach 39% of total sales. This is a very positive development for its diversification efforts that haven’t been appreciated by the investing community just yet, seeing as its share price continues to falter.

Wi-Fi 7 itself is still in early stages, with single-digit penetration, which means there is a massive potential for multi-year growth ahead for this company to further diversify from Apple. The management said that Broad Markets continues to gain momentum, especially in edge IoT, where Wi-Fi 7 adoption is seen to accelerate across many end-markets, like consumer, enterprise, and industrial. As of the latest conference call, the company’s CEO, Phil Brace, said that the “Broad Markets are expected to grow double-digit long-term, driven by Wi-Fi 7 adoption, automotive connectivity, and infrastructure opportunities.”

The management also expects upgrades to Wi-Fi 7 to persist for a couple of more years, as the backlog here is considerable, so the momentum to continue to perform well is strong.

On the topic of Wi-Fi 8, the company continues to work on the next-generation product, which will most likely take over from Wi-Fi7 at the end of 2026 and continue the success of its predecessor. As long as the company can win a lot more contracts over time, the broader market segment will make up more than half of the total revenues eventually.

Qorvo Merger

At the end of October last year, Skyworks announced it was looking to merge with Qorvo (QRVO) to create a $22B US-based RF/analog player. QRVO shareholders will get $32.5 in cash, and 0.960 in SWKS shares per share. This puts Qorvo’s value at around $13.5B equity. The news was received very well by many outlets and analysts, some of which even upgraded the two companies to overweight.

So, there must be some powerful synergies to come out of this, right? First of all, Qorvo essentially has the same problem as SWKS. In that, a lot of its revenues rely heavily on the same customer, Apple. So, is it really diversification? I would say yes. Its reliance on Apple isn’t as high as for Skyworks, which means overall, the Apple concentration will come down to around 45%-55%, much better than around 70% pre-merger.

Additionally, the merger is said to help SWKS to achieve over $500m in annual cost savings once fully integrated, which is expected in 2-2 and a half years down the line. Furthermore, the company expects to see EPS improvements immediately after the close.

The end-markets in focus after the merger will be sectors like defense, IoT, AI, and Auto.

This $2.6B Broad Market platform will target long-cycle, high-margin secular growth in the mentioned segments, which will continue to reduce Apple’s exposure to closer to 50% of sales.

There has been some panic in the markets recently regarding the merger, which was apparently driven by investor anxiety regarding the merger ahead of the Feb 11th Skyworks shareholder vote. The deal is far from over still. It faces some regulatory scrutiny from multiple regulatory entities. The biggest concern among the entities is China’s SAMR. The process is expected to be rather lengthy, lasting 6-12+ months due to US-China tensions, but preliminary views seem to lean on SAMR not vetoing the merger. There might be a relevant cautionary tale, however. Back in 2022, Intel (INTC) announced its intent to acquire an Israel-based foundry, Tower Semiconductor (TSEM), for around $5.4B, which eventually failed to materialize because China’s SAMR never gave it the approval after a lengthy review process of over a year. However, that situation was a bit different since the acquisition was between the US and Israel, while SWKS and QRVO are US-US, which are viewed as complementary and favor clearance, just like AMD’s (AMD) acquisition of Xilinx back in 2022.

The same thing goes for FTC. Since the products of the two companies are relatively different, there is very little overlap, which means monopoly risks are minimal, and this should pass the regulatory hurdles, too.

Valuation

Let's take a look at an updated valuation model, since it has been a while. Below are the assumptions I used.

I believe 8% over the next 5 years is very doable, with a refresh cycle of smartphones and other consumer electronics still to come, as well as inorganic growth. Margins of 21%, I think, are still a little too conservative, but it is better to be safe than sorry. D&A seems to be high, but that is its historical number from what I can see. Furthermore, I went with 9.5% WACC as my discount rate and 2.5% as my terminal growth rate. Below are the EPS projections we get, which are very close to what analysts are projecting.

With that said, the company continues to trade at a decent discount to its conservative fair value of around $90 a share.

Closing Comments

You can clearly see the theme of the article here: diversification efforts are well on their way, and with that, I think the company is still a good buy for those investors who are looking at the long-term picture. I believe that Skyworks is going to be much quicker at reducing Apple’s reliance than Apple is to replace Skyworks products completely. These could be famous last words, but I’ll stand by them.

The company’s share price is a lot more enticing than it was before, so I am very much tempted to start building up my position over the next couple of quarters. I will be monitoring the situation regarding the merger and future efforts of diversification, but I think it is looking quite positive.

data.title: Amazon Leo Could Be A Catalyst That Gets The Stock Moving
data.description:
Introduction

Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) launched Project Kuiper in 2019, with the goal of establishing a constellation of over 3200 satellites that provide fast and affordable internet access globally. In November, Project Kuiper was rebranded to Amazon Leo, as the company prepares to commercialize the service. With excitement around space on the rise and space stocks seemingly breaking out, Amazon Leo and AMZN's place in ARK Space & Defense Innovation ETF (ARKX) could serve as a spark for Amazon’s next leg higher.

From Project Kuiper to Amazon Leo

To date, Amazon has successfully launched at least 180 satellites into orbit. Per their agreement with the FCC, Amazon is required to launch 50% of the approved satellites by July of 2026 meaning over the next six months Amazon would need to launch over 1400 satellites into orbit. This is ambitious and it is likely that Amazon will be requesting an extension from the FCC. However, by the end of Q1, they expect to be in five countries: Germany, France, Canada, the United Kingdom, and the United States. By the end of 2027 Amazon expects to be offering Leo in 57 countries and close to 100 countries by the end of 2028. Although Amazon intends to offer Leo to the general public, it is not entirely clear yet when Leo will be available for use by the general public due to Amazon’s enterprise focused commercialization strategy.

Amazon’s stated roll out strategy is focused on enterprise users. They announced as part of their last earnings report that they’ve signed commercial agreements with JetBlue, Australia’s National Broadband Network, and Kazakhtelecom. Additionally, in 2025 they allowed certain businesses to begin testing the product. I like this strategy for three reasons:

  1. Leo currently outpaces Starlink in download speeds which should give them an advantage with enterprise users.
  2. Enterprise users will have a higher willingness and ability to pay compared to individual users.
  3. Because of AWS, Amazon already has the necessary channels in place and demonstrated ability to sell Leo to enterprise customers.

Once they establish the capacity to go beyond enterprise and offer Leo to the general public (which may still be a couple years out), it is the same story there. The sales channel is already in place with Prime. It is not clear what the initial pricing or margins on Leo will look like. However, regardless of how it performs financially on a standalone basis, Leo will have a positive impact on the other aspects of Amazon's business.

High speed internet is an unmet need for roughly 32% of the global population. There are currently 2.6 billion people across the planet without it. The FCC estimates that in the U.S. alone there are still 26 million people without highspeed internet access, so this is not just an issue in undeveloped nations.

Amazon expanding global high speed Internet access to these underserved populations and remote areas could strengthen and grow every other area of their business. On the surface, Leo may not look like a big deal but it may turn out to be a key enabler underpinning Amazon’s next decade of growth. The idea is pretty simple at its core. Leo allows more customers to use Amazon's services and adds to the flywheel bringing customers into the Amazon ecosystem. Because of Leo, more organizations can grow and build using AWS and more consumers can enjoy the benefits of Prime and Alexa. Leo could also supercharge Amazon’s advertising business. As Leo grows as an internet service provider, it could unlock a treasure trove of real time browsing data, serve as a constant source of fuel for advertising. As a stand-alone program, Leo may lose money or breakeven, but that is acceptable if it drives significant upside across Amazon’s other business segments.

The Technical Backdrop

The technicals are not amazing as there is some resistance to get through, but they are showing some positive signs of life. Amazon, after a yearlong consolidation, has been making higher lows since October and sales volume has been declining since November. The 150-day moving average is back above the 200-day moving average. The 50, 150, and 200 day moving average lines are all trending slightly upwards again and the stock continues to close above the 50 day. As a long-term investor, these are the technical factors I pay attention to, but they are not the be all end all. Do what works for you.

The Macroeconomic Backdrop

Both fiscal and monetary policy are aligned. On the monetary front, the federal reserve began buying T-Bills and we can expect a dovish federal reserve chair to be in place by May. On the fiscal front, the Trump administration is teasing stimulus checks, and the One Big Beautiful Bill Act just brought back 100% research and development expensing while maintaining 100% bonus depreciation on qualified capital expenditures. Tax cuts, direct stimulus, and an expanding federal reserve balance sheet all leads to more dollars in the economy and some of those extra dollars will likely show up in Amazon's revenue.

The Fundamentals

Amazon reportedly sits at a 27x FWD P/E multiple. With a trailing EPS of $7.20. This prices in roughly 20% FWD EPS growth compared to the TTM. Seeking Alpha projects Amazons FWD EPS growth to be closer to 40%.

If that happens, it means Amazon is really trading at 23x FWD earnings. Whether Amazon is at 27 or 23x forward earnings, I don’t know. But either way there is significant room for margin expansion. With Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST) both trading at close to 40x forward earnings, Amazon looks like a value stock. Walmart and Costco traditionally have net income margins of about 3% and their earnings growth rates over the next 3-5 years are expected to be in the 7-10% range. Amazon, on the other hand, is expected to grow earnings at roughly 28% over the next 3-5 years and their profit margin is generally north of 6%. Maybe I’m missing something, but it seems like the multiples should be flipped.

Risks

In the short run, delays to the roll out of Leo present regulatory and negative headline risk. As Amazon will likely apply for an extension to the satellite launch timeline, this could create negative headlines. FCC could also deny the request. While it is highly unlikely that the FCC will pull the plug, it is still a possibility.

The next risk worth noting is that the broader rally in space stocks could break down and go from a buoy to a weight on Amazon's stock price. Some of these space stocks are highly speculative and risk appetite can shift pretty quickly. Additionally, President Trump said he aims to block the major defense companies from issuing dividends and buy backs until production speeds up which could spell the end of the rally in space stocks.

Looking at the competitive landscape, SpaceX's (SPACE) Starlink is an obvious threat that dampens excitement around Leo. Starlink has a massive head start. According to their most recent network update, they have over 7800 satellites in orbit and more that 6 million customers globally.

The other key risks to focus on in my opinion all center around the technical, economic, and fundamental factors that we discussed previously. The technical picture could breakdown. Earnings growth could slow. We could get negative tariff news or inflation could rise beyond expectation and reverse the current macroeconomic trend.

Conclusion

This year long consolidation period in Amazon’s stock has been a blessing for long term investors in the midst of an impressive, index level bull market. But I think the days of consolidation are thankfully numbered. During this period, revenue grew double digits, margins expanded and earnings accelerated all while Amazon continued to invest for the future. Amazon’s Project Kuiper is now Amazon Leo, signaling that Amazon’s marketing department is getting involved as the company prepares for commercialization. This is taking place as space stocks look to be on the verge of a breakout. While this catalyst may provide the initial lift, the positive technical, macroeconomic and fundamental set up will help carry the day for AMZN's next leg of growth.

I hate price targets, but let’s have a little fun. If you take Seeking Alpha’s 40% forward earnings growth projection, and apply Costco’s multiple of 40x, Amazon is a $400 stock by the end of 2026. This is best-case scenario but it doesn’t take much imagination to get there. This represents roughly a 65% upside from here so the stock could fall drastically short of this and still make investors very happy. I don’t know what the year will bring, but I do believe the odds are in Amazon’s favor to significantly outperform the index, making it a strong buy for 2026.

data.title: TSMC Q4 Preview: Bullish, But Memory Inflation Could Break The 2026 Growth Story
data.description:
In my last coverage on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), I reiterated a strong buy into Q3 earnings on the view that AI/HPC-driven demand and tight leading-edge capacity would hold up the bull case.

My view has largely played out, and TSM is up double digits since my last update, despite the underperformance of the tech sector (XLK) in the US.

With Q4 earnings around the corner (January 15), I considered revisiting my bull thesis. Overall, I am not worried about the top-line results, as December sales data were strong. For Q4 2025, revenue was already disclosed, and the figure came in above the midpoint of management's guidance and the Street's estimates.

The key item to monitor is profitability. Gross margin needs to stay close to 60%, with overseas-fab dilution staying within the 1–2% FY25 benchmark. I don't have any compelling reasons to believe margins will come in below estimates.

However, there is a big risk that has recently lurked, and I believe I found an easy way to monitor its impact on the company.

Q4 2025 Earnings Preview

As is often the case with TSM, the market already knows a meaningful piece of Q4 performance before the full earnings release. This is because the company reports monthly revenue data.

On January 9, the company reported December 2025 revenue of NT$335.0B (up 20.45% YoY) and FY2025 revenue of NT$3,809.05B (up 31.6% YoY).

After adding up the November and October top-line figures, Q4 revenue totals NT$1.046T ($33.11 billion), representing a 5.7% sequential increase. In contrast, management’s prior Q4 revenue guidance was US$32.2B–US$33.4B. So, Q4 revenue came in above the midpoint of management guidance and also above the Street's estimate of $32.79 billion.

Therefore, on the top line, everything is fine. What I would closely monitor in the upcoming earnings release is margins. Specifically, management guided Q4 gross margins in the range of 59-61% and operating margins between 49-51%.

The dominating narrative is that this is a multi-year supercycle in semis and not a short-term cyclical wave. Therefore, I want to see gross margins staying as close as possible to 60%. As a reference, I included below the gross margin trajectory for the past few years.

Another reason I'm keeping a close eye on margins is due to the impact from overseas fabs (particularly, from the Kumamoto fab in Japan). Here are the CFO's remarks on the expected impact for H2 and FY 2025:

While the cost of overseas fabs remain higher, thanks to the company's overall larger scale, we now expect the gross margin dilution from the ramp-up of our overseas fabs to be closer to 2% in the second half of 2025. For the full year 2025, we now expect it to be between 1% to 2% as compared to 2% to 3% previously.

Another factor to monitor is the revenue mix. In Q3, HPC revenue accounted for 57% of total consolidated revenue. This is the growth driver of the company, so I expect the mix to remain close to 60% of total revenue.

Growth Expectations May Be Revised To The Upside

In the last earnings release, management reaffirmed an AI data center CAGR in the mid-40s between 2024 and 2029. Based on the CEO's remarks, it seems that they may have been too conservative with this estimate:

So the CAGR we -- previously we announced is about mid-40s. It's still -- it's a little bit better than that. We will update you probably in beginning of next year. So we have a more clear picture. Today, the number are insane.

Those “insane” numbers are likely going to lead to a spectacular 2026 guide. Therefore, I expect another jump in the Street's sales and EPS expectations for the next 4 quarters, as the new data centers will need to be filled with AI chips.

More often than not, the share price follows earnings expectations. Take a look at the chart below, showing the next 12 months' EPS consensus vs. price.

Some analysts project 30% revenue growth in 2026, citing N3 demand, N2 ramp, advanced packaging upside, and blended ASP uplift. I believe FY26 revenue growth could come in higher than 30% if the next risk doesn't materialize.

Risks

Back in Q3, smartphone revenue increased double digits yoy, accounting for 30% of the revenue mix. Let me explain why I'm sweating about the outperformance of the smartphone segment.

The memory market is hot (red-iron hot, to be precise). Just take a look at the returns of Micron and Sandisk over the past 6 months:

The players in the memory space (both DRAM and NAND) have a massive advantage from a pricing perspective, given the high demand for HBM for AI data center chips. The high demand for HBM memory is squeezing the supply chain, leading to shortages, especially for memory chips that are not used in data centers but are used in (you guessed it) smartphones.

Some sources suggest that Samsung and SK hynix have pitched Q1 server DRAM pricing up 60%–70% vs. Q4 to major clients, including Microsoft and Google. In fact, back in November, Reuters reported an earlier wave where Samsung raised prices on some server memory chips by 30%–60% as shortages worsened.

Here is a table from TrendForce that is not particularly reassuring (for investors outside of the memory chip space):

I strongly believe that the hike in memory costs will be passed to consumers. This could lead to a smartphone demand cool-off, which will impact TSM's top-line results.

I believe such an impact could be concentrated in the N3 process nodes (given that TSMC explicitly positions N3 as optimized for smartphones and HPC). However, I think any weakness would appear first in the more mature nodes (N4/N5 families), given that a very large share of flagship Android and premium-tier silicon has been made on the N4/N5 variants.

As for the older nodes (the N6/N7 family), a revenue decline does not necessarily mean weaker consumer demand. Take the example of the N7 contribution to wafer revenue over the last few years.

In this case, some customers may simply choose to upgrade to a newer process node, which is the main driver of the revenue decline.

Valuation

The stock has rerated over the last 12 months, and the P/E multiple is now 27x.

Bear in mind that, due to the earnings growth, the stock is cheaper today than in December 2024 (29x P/E), even though the share price is 53% higher.

On top of that, investors are paying 30x for next year's earnings. Is that cheap? Well, take a look below at the Street's top-line estimates for the next few quarters.

Call me crazy, but 30%+ EPS growth for the next three quarters justifies a 30x forward P/E multiple.

Conclusion

The setup makes sense for a multiple re-rate, and the chart supports an entry point, given the recent breakout to ATHs (followed by a retest).

Therefore, I reiterate my strong buy rating.

Looking ahead, I would closely monitor Q4 margins and the overseas-fab situation. Management’s last view is that overseas fabs dilute gross margin by 2% in H2’25, and 1–2% for FY25 is the benchmark. Any drift beyond that challenges my rerate logic.

data.title: TECL: Repeat Of 2025 Buying Opportunity Is Possible (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The AI boom is arguably defining the market so far this decade. For those who believe in the continued tech rally, it is tempting to capitalize on the surge in the tech sector by considering a leveraged ETF such as the Direxion Daily Technology Bull 3X Shares ETF (TECL). Investors betting on a continued surge in the tech sector, driven by the AI boom, may be blindsided by emerging risks, such as the rise of a second global tech ecosystem led by China, which threatens to reduce the size of the US-dominated tech ecosystem worldwide. The TECL ETF is off from its all-time highs, which makes it an enticing potential investment opportunity on the surface. Taking a broader view, with external risk factors seemingly intensifying, it would take a selloff similar to the one experienced last year during the tariff tantrum to make the risk/reward look enticing. Odds of such a selloff this year are high in my view; therefore, it is worth keeping this ETF in mind as an opportunity to buy on the dip.

Risks Associated With Leveraged ETFs

For an understanding of the risks associated with a leveraged ETF, I would like to direct the reader to the SEC's description of the potential pitfalls involved. As per the SEC description of the risk of leveraged ETFs, I want to directly quote the most relevant aspect of their warning.

" Some investors might invest in these ETFs with the expectation that the ETFs may meet their stated daily performance objectives over the long term as well. Investors should be aware that performance of these ETFs over a period longer than one day can differ significantly from their stated daily performance objectives and may potentially expose investors to significant and sudden losses."

I want to emphasize this description of the risks involved because it also provides a clear description of what to expect in terms of returns on a prolonged investment position, beyond the daily 3x performance up or down.

The best way to visualize the risks involved, as well as the return potential, is to recall the dramatic, roughly 2/3 drop in the share price that the TECL ETF experienced in the early months of 2025.

By the end of the year, the TECL ETF still outperformed the NASDAQ Composite (COMP:IND); however, investors need to be prepared for the volatility involved in such investments. In other words, if a deep selloff in the ETF, such as we saw in the Spring of 2025, were to occur, investors need to be prepared to wait out a recovery unless they are willing to take significant losses.

It should also be noted that just because the TECL ETF outperformed the NASDAQ composite last year, it does not mean that it will always be the case. If the NASDAQ composite were to have a down year, the TECL ETF will have a significantly worse year, comparatively speaking. The assumption that it will eventually outperform the NASDAQ composite is based on another assumption, namely that stock markets always go up in the long term. It took the Nikkei index three and a half decades to recover to its prior all-time highs reached at the end of 1989. There is no guarantee that US stock markets will not at some point suffer a similar long-term slump.

My Prior Coverage Of The TECL ETF

I last covered this ETF in March of last year. I had it then as a buy, given that it was down significantly from its all-time high. I continued buying as it declined further in April on the Trump tariffs issue, then I gradually sold the recovery in its share price. The main reason I now have it as a hold instead of a buy is that the recovery since the Spring of 2025 has broader markets pushing frothy valuations, with the S&P 500 currently trading at a P/E ratio of over 31, up from under 25 in March of 2025. Tech stocks are the main driver of higher market valuations. As long as valuations are higher, downside risks remain more pronounced.

About The TECL ETF

The TECL ETF is designed to move in correlation, at an amplified rate relative to its component stocks, by a factor of 3X daily, whether it is up or down. As the chart I provided above shows, it does not mean that it will outperform the tech-heavy Nasdaq by a factor of three measured on a longer timeline. The ETF is most closely correlated to the movements in the NASDAQ composite, but it is more specifically a reflection of the movement in the component stocks.

Its top holdings also reflect the Magnificent Seven stocks that currently drive the S&P index, which is why the P/E ratio of the S&P is a decent barometer of whether stocks represented in the TECL ETF are overpriced.

The ETF has an expense ratio of .91, and a dividend yield of 7%. Although it is not a dividend play by any means, it is nevertheless something worth considering when investing, given the generous distribution. The outperformance of the TECL ETF versus the NASDAQ Composite in the past year is partly due to its higher dividend payments.

Global Tech Is Splitting Into Two Ecosystems

As of right now, the market still views the global tech ecosystem as one large, all-encompassing entity. In other words, the market for most tech products is seen as global, with few significant barriers. Warnings of a technological split into two ecosystems, one dominated by the US and its partners, and another dominated by China's rising ecosystem, have been circulating for a while; however, there were some doubts on whether it can fully replace most foreign inputs in its supply chains.

News broke just before the end of the year that China now has a working prototype of its own lithography machine capable of producing high-end computer chips. I have been warning for some years now that ASML (ASML) is close to losing its near-monopoly in the field of chip manufacturing equipment. The market has been unresponsive so far to this increasingly plausible outcome. I attribute this in part to some skepticism about the efficiency of the machine and the ability of China to ramp up output of such machines in the near future.

I, personally, think that even if the machine is net inferior to ASML's machines, China will still favor using it over ASML out of geostrategic considerations. Being able to fully build out its own domestic ecosystem, complete with domestic supply chains meant to withstand coercive measures, may be more valuable than opting to use the best machines. Once its own ecosystem is built out, China can further improve through innovation, if it is indeed the case that its technology is somewhat inferior.

In terms of how fast China might be able to build out the domestic supply chains in support of this ambitious project, we have to keep in mind that China's industrial capacity, as well as its capacity to build out new capacities, is currently unmatched. China has shown an ability to withstand the pressures imposed on it since the first Trump term, meant to curtail its technological rise. The likes of Huawei returned to the phone market as a leaner, meaner competitor on the global tech scene, after it was temporarily prevented from making phones through curbs on crucial components. It is, in my view, a sign that it has deep resources to draw on.

My prognosis is that while the global tech market is a fast-growing pie, China will take a growing share of the pie, first domestically and then abroad. Its technologies do not even have to match or surpass ours on quality or even on price. The ever-present threat of access to technology being used as a geopolitical tool makes it desirable for most countries around the world to diversify their options, which can be a potent driving force in itself for global adoption of China's emerging independent tech ecosystem in many markets. What this means for most companies represented in the TECL ETF is that at some point in the future, perhaps as soon as this year, their future outlook will suffer a significant reduction, which will be followed by a sizable downward adjustment of their valuations, in other words, a potential broad-based tech selloff.

Other Headwinds

Aside from the growing schism of what used to be one global tech ecosystem into two or perhaps more in the future, there are other relevant questions when it comes to the current market enthusiasm toward the tech sector. The AI boom is yet to be fully supported by a high degree of monetization, which leaves the whole trend sitting on thin ice. There is also, in my view, a risk of IT saturation, which could be an unpredictable factor because no one really knows when enough might be enough at the consumer level as well as the business level.

I expect 2026 to be the year when we will see a great rotation out of the tech sector, into hard assets, such as mining, which is already evidenced in the form of the strong performance of gold & silver, as well as copper. Other segments of the mining sector are likely to follow. The simple dynamics of a segment outperforming another often tend to take on a life of their own and further deflate an already underperforming segment.

Investment Implications

Given the risk/reward considerations I outlined above, I intend to wait it out and only invest in the TECL ETF in the event of a significant pullback of 30%-40%. Even then, I intend to do it incrementally, as I have been doing in most cases in the past years. In other words, I intend to take up a small initial position and continue buying on the way down if it does continue to decline. If it does not decline further, I will be content to ride my initial position higher.

Investors need to keep an eye on this ETF because I foresee plenty of potential triggers for a broader market selloff this year, perhaps similar to the one we had in the Spring of 2025, on the back of uncertainties related to the tariff regime that the Trump administration introduced. It could be anything from more geopolitical uncertainty to bond yields rising on high deficits or other factors.

Risks To My Thesis

The obvious risk of sitting on the sidelines is that the tech sector rally continues, resulting in a potential opportunity missed. There are plenty of factors that can lead to a continued rally in the sector. Market momentum alone, if it is uninterrupted by events or company-specific negative results, can carry the ongoing tech sector rally forward. Better-than-expected company-specific as well as sectoral results could also push this ETF much higher in the coming months. While the risk of missing out is considerable, at this point, I also see a significant chance of a major downturn in the tech sector, which can lead to a very deep selloff in the TECL ETF; therefore, I prefer to exercise caution.

Conclusions

If the right combination of events produces the right buying opportunity, the TECL ETF can prove to be a great investment opportunity, as long as the entry and the exit strategies are thought through and planned correctly. For this reason, I am looking at a well-timed initial entry strategy, followed by incremental buying if it declines further. I intend to incrementally sell shares on the way up.

Because it is a risky investment instrument, I intend to keep my exposure capped well below my usual 3% of total portfolio levels I normally aim for, in the case of most stocks or ETFs. Timing is key when it comes to finding a good entry point that provides a solid risk/reward justification to invest. I believe there will be at least one opportunity to buy this ETF this year, and it is worth taking the time to keep a lookout for a good entry point because a well-timed investment can produce significant short-term gains, as was the case after last year's Spring stock market selloff.
